coredata.dc:title: Label-free carbon dots from black sesame seeds for real-time detection of ammonia vapor via optical electronic nose and density functional theory calculation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Supchocksoonthorn, Preeyanuch
Thongsai, Nichaphat
Moonmuang, Hataipat
Kladsomboon, Sumana
Jaiyong, Panichakorn
Paoprasert, Peerasak
coredata.dc:description: Synthesis of carbon dots from natural precursors and their use in novel sensing applications currently gain much attention in the carbon dot research community because of its practicality and scaling-up possibility. Herein, carbon dots were synthesized from black sesame seeds via hydrothermal method and used as a dual-mode probe for the detection of ammonia in vapor and solution phases. The size of carbon dots was around 7.6 nm, and they exhibited blue-color emission under UV excitation in solution with a quantum yield of 2%. For the first time, the obtained carbon dots as a sensing layer were then used in optical electronic nose for the real-time, room-temperature detection of ammonia vapor and several volatile organic compounds. Our carbon dots in optical electronic nose could sense ammonia vapor generated from ammonia aqueous solutions with a detection limit of 0.8%v/v. Furthermore, they could also differentiate ammonia in various mixtures, containing methanol and water and from ethylenediamine and triethylamine. The density functional theory calculations were used to confirm the change in optical absorbance of carbon dots when exposed to solvent vapors. The computational results supported the experimental findings well in which the carbon dots and ammonia formed the most stable complex among all vapor molecules computed. A paper-based device was also constructed to prove the practicality and versatility of our carbon dot-based ammonia sensors. In summary, solid-state optical absorption sensing via electronic nose has proved to be a real-time, convenient approach for the detection of ammonia vapor. This work also demonstrates experimentally and computationally that the carbon dots are unique nanomaterials and the ammonia sensors based on carbon dots will be useful in various fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Black sesame seed
Ammonia sensor
Electronic nose
Density functional theory

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from black sesame seed
Synthesis and characterizations of black sesame seed derived-carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for the preparation of carbon dots from black sesame seeds and their ammonia sensing applications.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of carbon dots, (b) survey XPS spectrum of carbon dots, (c) high-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C1s, (d) N1s, and (e) O1s regions.
(a) TEM image of carbon dots; inset figure shows magnified view of lattice spacing, (b) histogram of size distribution, (c) UV–vis absorbance, and (d) fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots with various concentrations of ammonia and (b) different pH environments. (c) Plot of fluorescence emission intensities and ammonia concentrations. (d) Images of paper-based sensors with various ammonia concentrations. (e) RGB values of paper-based sensors and (f) plot of red-green-blue (R+B+G) values with various concentrations of ammonia (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) Sensor signals using yellow light source and (b) PCA score plots of ammonia-water solutions, (c) plot of sensor signal magnitude and ammonia concentration, and (d) UV–vis spectra of carbon dots with (yellow line) and without (black line) ammonia vapor (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
(a) Sensor signals and (b) PCA score plots of standard ammonia and unknown solutions.

(a) Sensor signals from green light source and (b) PCA score plots of ammonia, ethylene diamine, and triethylamine. (c) The gas sensing response (G) of carbon dots with different vapor sources (ammonia, ethylene diamine, and triethylamine). (d) Sensor signals from green light source and (e) PCA score plots of ammonia, methanol, formaldehyde, and water. (f) G of carbon dots with ammonia, methanol, formaldehyde, and water vapor sources (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
(a) Sensor signal and (b) PCA score plots of ammonia-methanol solutions. (c) Sensor signal and (d) PCA score plots of ammonia-methanol-water solutions.

(a) Top-view of carbon dot-ammonia model and (b) side view of carbon dot-ammonia model. (c) Top-view and (d) side-view of carbon dot model and (e) calculated absorption spectra in gas phase and implicit solvents.
Calculated interaction energies and distances between carbon dot model and solvent molecules.

head.ce:title: Label-free carbon dots from black sesame seeds for real-time detection of ammonia vapor via optical electronic nose and density functional theory calculation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathumthani, 12120, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiological Technology, Faculty of Medical Technology, Mahidol University, Bangkok, 73170, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of carbon dots from natural precursors and their use in novel sensing applications currently gain much attention in the carbon dot research community because of its practicality and scaling-up possibility. Herein, carbon dots were synthesized from black sesame seeds via hydrothermal method and used as a dual-mode probe for the detection of ammonia in vapor and solution phases. The size of carbon dots was around 7.6 nm, and they exhibited blue-color emission under UV excitation in solution with a quantum yield of 2%. For the first time, the obtained carbon dots as a sensing layer were then used in optical electronic nose for the real-time, room-temperature detection of ammonia vapor and several volatile organic compounds. Our carbon dots in optical electronic nose could sense ammonia vapor generated from ammonia aqueous solutions with a detection limit of 0.8%v/v. Furthermore, they could also differentiate ammonia in various mixtures, containing methanol and water and from ethylenediamine and triethylamine. The density functional theory calculations were used to confirm the change in optical absorbance of carbon dots when exposed to solvent vapors. The computational results supported the experimental findings well in which the carbon dots and ammonia formed the most stable complex among all vapor molecules computed. A paper-based device was also constructed to prove the practicality and versatility of our carbon dot-based ammonia sensors. In summary, solid-state optical absorption sensing via electronic nose has proved to be a real-time, convenient approach for the detection of ammonia vapor. This work also demonstrates experimentally and computationally that the carbon dots are unique nanomaterials and the ammonia sensors based on carbon dots will be useful in various fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials offer unique optical and electronic properties for sensing, optoelectronics, and biomedical applications [1,2]. Carbon dots, as carbon-rich nanoparticles, are regarded as one of the most widely studied nanomaterials to date since their discovery in 2004 in the Professor Scrivens’ laboratory [3]. The structure and compositions of carbon dots determine their diverse properties. Carbon dots have outstanding merits in terms of small size, high water solubility [4], good photostability [5], excellent biocompatibility [6], straightforward surface functionalization [7], and excitation-dependent or tunable emission [8]. They have been prepared from a variety of source materials using solvothermal-based techniques in a range of aqueous conditions. Normally, acids are added as dehydrating agents to help transform the precursors to carbon dots through dehydration and carbonization processes. In addition, the presence of acids in reaction mixture leads to the formation of hydrophilic functional groups on carbon dot surface, such as hydroxyls, carboxyls, and carbonyls via hydrolysis and oxidation reactions. These functional groups make the carbon dots highly soluble in aqueous media and have been called as surface states responsible for the unique photoluminescent emission of the carbon dots. In addition, these groups are responsible for attractive sensing ability of carbon dots as they can bind to small molecules or can be modified to improve the selectivity and sensitivity. Carbon dots have been therefore used as chemical and bio-probes in a number of sensing applications [9,10].

One of the latest trends for the preparation of carbon dots focuses on the use of readily available natural resources as precursors. Various natural resources are abundant, cheap, and renewable. In this work, black sesame seeds were used as precursor for the synthesis of carbon dots via hydrothermal method. Black sesame seeds are rich in fats, carbohydrates, and proteins, suitable to be a carbon dot precursor [11]. Previously, V. Roshni et al. reported the use of sesame seed-derived for Fe(III) sensing [12]. We aimed to extend further by employing the as-prepared carbon dots from black sesame seeds without any passivation or functionalization for sensing of ammonia vapor via optical electronic nose. Frequently, carbon dots have been used as fluorescence sensing probes, but their optical absorptivity is often neglected. Carbon dots are stronger UV absorber with long tailing into visible region. Herein, we exploited the optical absorbance of carbon dots for the detection of ammonia vapors via the integration with optical electronic nose. Electronic nose, mimicking the human olfactory system, consists of multisensory array that is sensitive to gaseous chemicals and can produce electronic responses upon exposure to gases and thus capable of detecting odors, vapors, gases, and volatile compounds [13–15]. For electronic nose used in this study, optical absorption/transmission of the carbon dot sensing film was monitored upon exposure to ammonia vapor and attractively the operation was carried out at room temperature. Ammonia is an ingredient in fertilizer, explosive, and considered as biomarker related to several diseases, such as peptic ulcers and renal/liver diseases [16–18]. Some bacteria, such as S. aureus and P. aeruginosa release ammonia as a major volatile compound [19]. Ammonia is irritant and corrosive with pungent odor and can cause cornea swelling, halo vision, and pulmonary edema. Exposure to ammonia at high concentrations can result in life-threatening situation. According to the European Agency for Safety and Health at Work, the occupation exposure limits for ammonia are 20 ppm for 8 h daily exposure and 50 ppm for acute exposure. Meanwhile, based on the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) in the USA, the exposure limits are 35 ppm for 15 min and 25 ppm for 8 h [20]. Therefore, detection of ammonia vapor is useful for detection of pollutant and gas leaks for environmental protection, explosive and fertilizer industries, compressor of air conditioners, breath analysis for medical diagnosis, and animal housing.

A number of ammonia vapor sensors have been fabricated using a variety of sensing materials, such as conjugated polymers [21], metal oxides [22], and carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene [23] and carbon nanotube [24]. Despite their high sensitivity, these materials require tedious preparation procedures. Conjugated polymers are susceptible to be oxidized under ambient operating conditions and metal oxide-based sensors can operate only at high temperatures to activate the adsorption and desorption of ammonia vapor, leading to the use of high power consumption and shorten sensor life [25]. Therefore, carbon dots are excellent alternative choice over the aforementioned materials for selective detection of ammonia vapor because of their ease of synthesis [26], tunable optical properties [27], and the ability to probe small chemicals [28]. To the best of our knowledge, there are very few reports on using carbon dots as ammonia sensors. For example, Travlou et al. reported their sulfur-doped carbon dots from poly(sodium-4-styrene)-based polymers that showed excellent sensitivity towards ammonia [29]. Jiang et al. reported the fabrication of sugarcane-derived carbon dots for detection of ammonia [30]. These two previous studies were however based on measuring fluorescence emission quenching of carbon dots in ammonia solution. Herein, for the first time we described the novel use of unmodified carbon dots to sense ammonia vapor by monitoring the optical transmission via optical electronic nose. Monitoring optical transmission of carbon dots for sensing is advantageous because the optical absorption is less sensitive to reaction conditions, and therefore, there is no need to maximize the quantum yields of the carbon dots or tediously optimize reaction conditions. Furthermore, quantum yields of carbon dots, especially those synthesized from natural raw materials, often vary from batch to batch. The integration between carbon dots and electronic nose is thus promising because of their compact sizes, convenience, and precision and the operation can be carried out at ambient conditions [31,32]. With the use of principal component analysis (PCA), a statistical tool commonly employed to reduce high dimension data into orthogonal principal components, our carbon dots were able to differentiate ammonia vapor from methanol and water vapors, binary and ternary mixtures between ammonia, methanol, and water. A concentration of a commercial ammonia sample from drugstore was accurately determined. We also employed the carbon dots to sense ammonia in aqueous solutions by monitoring their florescence intensity and fabricated the paper-based ammonia sensors. The density functional theory (DFT) calculations were used to investigate the unique interaction between ammonia and carbon dots. Based on the time-dependent DFT calculation, the optical absorption of carbon dots increased when exposed to ammonia, consistent with the electronic results. From these findings, carbon dots from black sesame seeds have shown novel properties for ammonia vapor sensing, which will benefit various applications in chemical, biomedical, and pharmaceutical fields.

All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich unless otherwise noted. Black sesame seed were purchased from a local supermarket. The dialysis membrane with molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1000 Da was bought from Membrane Filtration Product, Inc. All solutions were prepared and diluted by deionized (DI) water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from black sesame seed
ce:section.ce:para: Water-cleaned, dried black sesame seeds (4 g) and 0.5 M sulfuric acid solution (50 mL) were placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The solution was filtered, neutralized with 10 M NaOH solution, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The carbon dot solution was further purified using dialysis membrane (1000 Da MWCO) for 72 h and freeze dried to yield the brown solid product as carbon dot powder (0.1544 g, 3.8%).

The relative quantum yields of the carbon dots were measured based on our previous studies [31,32]. Briefly, quinine sulfate was used as a reference. The optical absorbance and emission of carbon dots and quinine sulfate were measured and the quantum yield was calculated using the slope method.

The carbon dot powder was characterized using a Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Spectrum 2000) and an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS; AXIS ULTRADLD, Kratos analytical, Manchester, UK). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured using a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV, at a magnification of 100,000 × . The carbon dot solution (0.01 g L−1) was characterized using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-1700 PharmaSpec), a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Jasco, FP-6200), and a Nano Particle Analyzer (HORIBA, Nano Partica SZ-100. Temperature, scattering angle, refractive index, viscosity, pH, and conductivity of solution are 25 °C, 173°, 1.33, 0.894 mPa·s, 12, and 31.327 mS cm−1, respectively.

The carbon dot film was prepared on a cleaned 2.5 × 2.5 cm2 microscope glass slide by drop-casting a carbon dot aqueous solution (1 mL, 30 g L−1) after ultrasonication for 20 min. The film was then allowed to dry at 60 °C overnight. Following our previous reports [31,32], the electronic nose setup is depicted in Fig. 1 . Our optical electronic nose system consists of eight LED light sources, including infrared, red (638 nm), yellow (587 nm), green (537 nm), blue (457 nm), blue-green (472 nm), violet (399 nm), and white, photodetector, and data acquisition unit. Further details can be found in our previous work [33].

The ammonia sensing ability of the carbon dots was investigated using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The carbon dot solution (1.5 mL, 0.1 mg L−1) was equally added to six different concentrations of ammonia to prepare the carbon dot/ammonia mixture with final concentrations of ammonia of 0, 1.25, 2.5, 10, 22.5% v/v and equal total volume. The solutions were then measured for the fluorescence intensity using a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The Whatman No.1 paper filter (1 × 1 cm2) was immersed in carbon dot solution (3 g L−1). Different concentrations of ammonia solution (0.00, 0.50, 1.00, 1.25, 1.50, 2.00, 2.50, 10, 22.5, and 30.0% v/v) were then dropped onto the carbon dot-immersed paper, and the sensing ability was observed by illumination under a UV lamp (365 nm). Pictures of paper-based sensor were taken using iPhone 6S Plus.

All calculations were carried out using the Gaussian 09 program package. A graphitic flake substituted with graphitic nitrogen on the basal plane and oxygen functional groups at the edge (C52H18N5O9) was used as the carbon dot model which was constructed based on FT-IR and XPS results. The complexes were built by placing the ammonia, methanol, formaldehyde, and water molecules on top of the graphitic fragment. All proposed structures were first optimized using semiempirical molecular orbital package (MOPAC) with Hamiltonian of PM6-D3H4 and followed by the geometry optimization with density functional theory (DFT) using the Minnesota M06-2X functional with def2-SVP basis set and fine integral grid. After the structure optimization, the interaction energies of the optimized structures were calculated using the DFT/M06-2X/def2-TZVPP with counterpoise correction method. The interaction energies between the carbon dot and vapor molecules were calculated based on Eq. (1):
(1)
I . E .
=
E
c o m p l e x
- (
E
c a r b o n
d o t
+
E
v a p o r
)
where Ecomplex is the total energy of the complex, Ecarbon dot is the energy of carbon dot, and Evapor is the energy of vapor molecule.

Based on previous work [33], the first 20 excited states of carbon dot model (C35H16N4O9) with bulk solvent effect were computed using time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) with M06-2X functional, def2-TZVPP basis set, and polarizable continuum model (PCM). The absorption spectra were subsequently generated using a GaussSum Version 3.0 program.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of black sesame seed derived-carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, carbon dots were synthesized from black sesame seeds as a carbon source. Black sesame seeds, a source of sesame oil and seasoning in many delicacies, are rich in fats, carbohydrates, and proteins [11]. They are known to provide health benefits, such as high calcium content [34], anti-aging [35], lowering cancer risk [36], etc. Due to their high content of carbon rich materials, readily availability, and environmental friendliness, the sesame seed is suitable for carbon dot synthesis [12]. Synthesis of carbon dots from natural resources is also a practical approach for the large-scale production. In this study, the black sesame seeds were subjected to a hydrothermal process in a Teflon-lined autoclave reactor. Hydrothermal method is one of the most widely used green synthesis methods for carbon dots due to its convenience and ability to produce high-quality and high-yield carbon dots [37]. Sulfuric acid is a dehydrating agent catalyzing the carbonization reaction and inducing the formation of hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of carbon dots. The black sesame seeds underwent hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 4 h, which yielded the best quantum yield and product yield of carbon dots. After neutralization, centrifugation, and purification by dialysis, the carbon dot solution was collected and showed blue emission under UV excitation (Fig. 2 ). The freeze-dried carbon dots were then used for characterizations and as amine sensors both in vapor and solution phases.

First, the purified carbon dots were characterized using FT-IR spectroscopy to determine surface functional groups. The FT-IR spectrum showed a broad vibration band at 3372 cm−1, corresponding to OH and NH stretching vibrations (Fig. 3 a). Vibration signals at 2963, 1648, 1137, and 617 cm-1 corresponded to CH stretching, CC/CO stretching, CO stretching, and CS stretching, respectively, indicating that the carbon dots contained hydroxyl, carboxyl, and alkenyl groups [38–41]. The presence of these oxygenated functional groups enhanced the solubility and stability of carbon dots in aqueous solutions. These groups are also reported to be the emissive sites responsible for fluorescence emission of carbon dots [42]. Next, the carbon dots were characterized using XPS spectroscopy. The XPS survey spectrum of carbon dots exhibited intense signals of C1s, N1s, and O1s at 284.9 eV, 399.9 eV, and 531.9 eV, with atomic concentrations of 67.2%, 9.0%, and 22.4%, respectively (Fig. 3b). Our carbon dots are therefore considered to be nitrogen-doped. High-resolution C1s spectrum showed signals at 284.4 eV, 286.5 eV, 287.8 eV, and 289.6 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, COC, CO, and OCO, respectively (Fig. 3c). The high resolution of the N1s peak showed the presence of C-NH2, pyrrolic nitrogen, quaternary nitrogen, and nitrogen oxide at 398.6 eV, 399.9 eV, 401.7 eV, and 402.7 eV, respectively (Fig. 3d). The high resolution of the O1s peak confirmed the existence of CO, C–O, OCO, and COC at 531.2 eV, 532.5 eV, 533.7 eV, and 535.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 3e) [43–47]. Both FT-IR and XPS results are in a good agreement that the carbon dots are carbon-rich and consist of various oxygen-containing functional groups and sp2 carbons and nitrogen.

The TEM technique was used to characterize the morphology and size of carbon dots. The TEM images of carbon dots exhibited scattered quasi-spherical nanoparticles with a uniform dispersion and a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm (Fig. 4 a). Particle diameters ranged from 4 to 12 nm with an average size of 7.6 nm (Fig. 4b). The blue emission and size of about 7.6 nm of our carbon dots were consistent with previous works [48–50]. The zeta potential of neutral carbon dot solution was measured to be -23.3 mV, which is quite highly negative for carbon dots from natural precursors. This highly negative zeta potential suggests a good dispersion of nanoparticles, as also shown in the TEM image. The zeta potential of negative value possibly arises because the carbon dots are rich in oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups as being identified from FT-IR and XPS characterizations.

The optical absorption and emission properties of carbon dots were characterized using UV–vis and fluorescence emission spectroscopy. The UV–vis spectrum showed strong optical absorption in UV region (200–300 nm) with long tailing into the visible region up to 600 nm A small absorption peak at 270 nm is due to the π- π* transition from the sp2 carbons in the carbon dots, consistent with FT-IR and XPS results (Fig. 4c). The fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots were measured as a function of excitation wavelength and displayed an excitation dependence emission. As the excitation wavelength was increased from 320 to 480 nm, the emission wavelength was gradually red-shifted. The maximum emission at 460 nm was obtained under excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 4d). When excited at 480 nm, the emission wavelength was in the range of 490–590 nm, which is in green-color region. This excitation-dependent property indicates that the carbon dots can be used as multi-color fluorophores. This observation is believed to arise from multiple emissive sites, e.g., conjugated core and surface functional groups as their emission wavelength and intensity depend on the excitation wavelength [51–54]. The relative quantum efficiency of the carbon dots was determined to be 2% using quinine sulfate as a reference. The long-term stability or shelf life of the carbon dot solution was also investigated. We found that the fluorescence emission intensity of carbon dot solution remained above 90% after 8 weeks (Fig. S1a, Supplementary Information). The photobleaching experiment was carried out by measuring the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission of the carbon dots under continuous UV irradiation (24 W, 365 nm). It was found that the fluorescence emission and absorbance retained over 80% after 6 h irradiation (Fig. S1b, Supplementary Information). These results suggest that our black sesame seed-derived carbon dots are appreciably photostable. The photostability of carbon dots was tested in various aqueous environments including NaCl and PBS buffer solutions. The fluorescence emission intensity was relatively unchanged in NaCl and PBS buffer solutions of different concentrations (Fig. S1c and S1d, Supplementary Information). These photostability experiments thus confirm that our carbon dots have sufficient stability suitable for practical applications.

First, we exploited our black sesame seed-derived carbon dots for the detection of ammonia solution. In this study, we took advantage of fluorescence emission properties of carbon dots because the fluorescence emission of carbon dots depends the surrounding environment. The fluorescence emission spectra were then measured as a function of ammonia concentrations in aqueous media (Fig. 5 a). As the concentration of ammonia in solution was increased, the fluorescence emission intensity increased. When the carbon dots were dissolved in KOH solutions at different pH, the fluorescence emission intensity dropped (Fig. 5b). This confirmed that the fluorescence emission enhancement by ammonia was not due to the effects of pH. Instead, ammonia passivated the surface of carbon dots, making them nitrogen-doped carbon dots which are known to have high fluorescence intensity. This result suggests that ammonia passivation can be a simple, alternative route for the preparation of fluorescence-enhanced carbon dots. The limit of detection was determined to be 0.8%v/v (Fig. 5c). A simple paper-based sensor was prepared from the carbon dot dip-coated filter paper. The paper sensor was then dipped in various concentrations of ammonia solutions and the images were taken of under UV excitation for red-green-blue (RGB) extraction (Fig. 5d). The RGB values were found to increase with increasing ammonia concentrations, consistent with the above results as higher ammonia concentrations led to the enhanced fluorescence emission intensity (Fig. 5e). The LOD value calculated from the RGB curve in the linear regime was 0.8%v/v, also consistent with the LOD value from the solution-based results (Fig. 5f).

We have demonstrated the use of carbon dots for the detection of ammonia in solution. Therefore, to demonstrate their versatility as unique, multifunctional nanomaterials, we next utilized them as probes for ammonia detection in vapor phase. Carbon dots are nanometer-sized and photostable optical nanomaterials and also possess strong UV–vis absorption in addition to the strong fluorescence emission and hydrophilic functional groups on the surface. Hence, the optical absorption properties of carbon dots were used in this experiment. Specifically, the carbon dot film, prepared by a simple drop-casting method, was used as a sensing probe in optical electronic nose for ammonia vapor sensing. Exposure to ammonia vapor at high concentrations and/or over a long period of time can severely damage vital organs such as eyes, skin, and respiratory tract. Ammonia is also flammable and can cause explosion when ignited. Detection of ammonia vapor is therefore strategically important in various fields.

The sensitivity of our carbon dot-integrated optical electronic nose was first tested towards ammonia vapor generated by flowing nitrogen gas through ammonia aqueous solutions. Ammonia-water mixtures (0, 7.5, 15, 22.5, and 30% v/v) were utilized as vapor sources. The sensor signal of the film, proportional to the optical transmission, was monitored as the nitrogen gas and solvent vapor were alternately flowed to the carbon dot film. The sensor signal of the film decreased immediately when the flow was switched from nitrogen gas to the ammonia-water solutions at all concentrations, confirming that the optical transmission of carbon dots was responsive to ammonia-water vapor (Fig. 6 a). When the gas flow was switched back to nitrogen gas, the sensor signal recovered to the original intensity. It recovered to the original value after the film had been exposed to nitrogen gas for 30 s. This suggests that our sensor can be used in a real time and produced relatively fast detection. The switch of gases could be repeated multiple times and the carbon dots exhibited appreciable repeatability for the detection of vapors. The magnitudes of sensor signal were higher when the concentration of ammonia in aqueous solutions was increased, indicating a higher sensitivity of the carbon dots towards ammonia than water. This result suggests that the change of optical transmission of carbon dots was due to the adsorption of ammonia on carbon dot surfaces, possibly through the polar-polar interactions between ammonia molecules and hydrophilic functional groups on carbon dots. An increase in optical absorption of carbon dots result agreed well with fluorescence emission enhancement in the presence of ammonia as shown above. Compared to previous reports, for example, Ganiga et al. developed FRET-sensing materials using carbon dots and sodium rhodizonate to detect ammonia in both solution and gas phase as low as 3 ppm. Travelogue et al. utilized sulfur-doped carbon dots to detect ammonia vapor in ppm range [29]. Although both reports show remarkably higher sensitivity than our carbon dots, our carbon dot-integrated electronic nose developed in this work provided a novel platform for the detection and sensing of ammonia vapor via the use of carbon dots derived from natural precursors without any modifications or the use of FRET pairs.

To further examine the selectivity of carbon dots towards different vapors, the sensor signals were analyzed using PCA. Through orthogonal transformation, the PCA method is widely used for reducing high dimensional data into linearly uncorrelated variables (principal components, PCs). The linearly uncorrelated variables were then plotted and called PCA score plots, showing relationship among data points. The sensor signals from the eight LEDs were used as the input data. These eight optical sensors were from the different LED light sources including infrared, red, yellow, green, violet, pink, blue, and white. A circle was used to mark the zone of each vapor source in the PCA score plot. No overlap between the PCA score plots at different concentrations was found, accounting for 99.7% of total variability (Fig. 6b). No overlapping indicates that the sensitivity of carbon dots toward different concentrations of ammonia solutions were statistically different. This result confirms that our carbon dot film in electronic nose can be employed to distinguish ammonia solutions at different concentrations. A Stern-Volmer-like plot was obtained by plotting the magnitude of sensor signal and ammonia concentration (Fig. 6c). The detection limit, determined using the 3σ/slope method where σ is the standard deviation from the pure water sample, was determined to be 4% v/v, meaning that our sensor can be used to detect ammonia concentration as low as 4% v/v with acceptable confidence level. The sensing ability of carbon dot toward ammonia was further investigated using UV–vis spectroscopy. The carbon dot film was prepared on a quartz slide and its absorbance was then measured in the absence and presence of ammonia vapor. An increase in absorbance was observed when the ammonia vapor was flowed through the film (Fig. 6d), indicating that the carbon dots absorbed more light when exposed to ammonia vapor. This was consistent with the electronic nose results that light transmission decreased with the flow of ammonia vapor. This study confirms that the sensing capability of our carbon dots was due to the change of the optical absorption upon exposure to vapors.

The ability of the carbon dots to detect a commercial ammonia sample (8% v/v ammonia specified on the bottle label) obtained from a drugstore against two standard ammonia solutions (8 and 20% v/v) was further investigated to demonstrate the accuracy and reliability of our sensor (Fig. 7 a). The PCA score plots of two standard solutions did not overlap, indicating the ability of carbon dots to discriminate vapors from the two solutions, and the score plot of the real ammonia sample overlapped well with 8% ammonia standard solution (Fig. 7b). This demonstrated the capability of the carbon dots of accurately determining the ammonia concentration of a real sample and also confirmed that the commercial ammonia sample is indeed about 8% v/v.

To further test the selectivity of our carbon dot-integrated electronic nose to discriminate ammonia from other highly polar solvents, methanol, formaldehyde, and water were used as the vapor sources along with ammonia solution. Methanol and water were chosen as representative solvents because of their high polarities like ammonia whereas ammonia and formaldehyde are both gases at room temperature. The sensor signal decreased dramatically when the flow was switched to all solvent vapors, albeit different magnitudes, confirming that the carbon dots were also sensitive to methanol, formaldehyde, and water vapors (Fig. 8 a). Different magnitudes of sensor signals indicated different sensitivities of our carbon dots towards different types of vapor. The PCA results confirmed that the carbon dots were able to clearly differentiate ammonia, formaldehyde, methanol, and water without overlapping among their score plots (Fig. 8b). We selected the first two principal components (PC1 and PC2(, on which the carbon dots were able to discriminate all four solvent vapors with 98.7% of the total variability. We further analyzed the sensitivity of carbon dots toward the solvents by calculating the gas sensing response. The gas sensing response (G) is basically the change of light intensity between vapor sample and reference gas with respect to the reference signal as shown in Eq. (2):
(2)
G =
S S
-
S R
S R
where SS is the sensor signal of the vapor sample and SR is the sensor signal of the reference (nitrogen) gas. Fig. 8c shows the comparison between gas sensing response of carbon dots upon adsorption of different vapor sources, and water. The highest sensing response was obtained with ammonia vapor (-6.18), following by methanol (-4.14), formaldehyde (-2.39), and water (-1.76). These results indicate that our carbon dot-integrated electronic nose has sufficient selectivity to classify vapors although highly polar solvents were used as vapor sources.

The sensitivity and selectivity of carbon dot-integrated electronic nose was further tested with different amines, including ammonia, ethylenediamine, and triethylamine. The sensor signals decreased when ammonia and triethylamine vapors were flowed through the carbon dots with a stronger response toward ammonia than triethylamine (Fig.8d and f). However, the electronic nose was not responsive to ethylenediamine possibly due to its lowest vapor pressure compared to those of ammonia and triethylamine. Different sensitivities of carbon dots towards these amine compounds were also confirmed by the PCA plots as the PCA plots did not show the overlapping (Fig. 8e). These results indicated that our sensor is capable of identifying ammonia from other types of amine compounds.

Since both ammonia and methanol are highly polar molecules, the sensitivity of our electronic nose towards ammonia-methanol mixtures (0, 25, 50, 75, and 100% v/v) as vapor sources was further studied. Similarly, the sensor signal of the carbon dots decreased significantly when the ammonia-methanol vapor was flowed to the carbon dot film. The sensor signals were stronger when the concentration of ammonia was increased, indicating a higher sensitivity of the carbon dots towards ammonia, consistent with the results above (Fig. 9 a). The PCA score plots showed no overlap at different concentrations, and there accounted for 96.2% of total variability (Fig. 9b). We also tested the sensitivity of our carbon dot-integrated electronic nose to characterize ternary ammonia-methanol-water mixtures (1:2:1, 1:1:2, and 2:1:1 by volume) as vapor sources (Fig. 9c). Similarly, no overlap was found between the PCA score plots at different concentrations, and there accounted for 97% of total variability (Fig. 9d). Based on these results, the carbon dot-integrated optical electronic nose can be used to detect ammonia and its mixture with methanol and water. Furthermore, the sensitivity depends upon the polarity and volatility of the solvent molecules. The change in optical transmission of the carbon dots upon exposure to vapors could be due to a change in environment around the carbon dots, produced by the solvent molecules, which were confirmed later by UV–vis results and DFT calculations.

Furthermore, we tested the ability of carbon dots thin film to detect methanol-water mixtures (0, 25, 50, 75, and 100% v/v) as vapor sources. The sensor signal of carbon dots decreased when the flow was switched to the methanol-water vapor (Fig.S2a, Supplementary Information). The optical electronic nose can distinguish various concentrations of methanol-water mixtures as shown in Fig. S2b, Supplementary Information. The magnitude of sensor signal was used to construct a linear plot. These plots showed a linear relationship between the sensor signal and methanol concentration, indicating that the methanol vapor played a role in modulating the optical transmission through the carbon dots (Fig. S2c, Supplementary Information) with the detection limit determined using the 3σ/slope method was 3.0%. In addition, this study indicates that our carbon dots can also be used to detect methanol vapor and thus can be considered as multi-faceted vapor sensor. Detection of methanol vapor will benefit various fields as methanol is one of the most used chemicals and building blocks for chemical synthesis. The UV–vis absorbance of carbon dot film was also measured in the absence and presence of methanol vapor. With methanol vapor, an increase in optical absorbance was observed (Fig. S2d, Supplementary Information). This was consistent with the electronic nose results that optical transmission decreased with the flow of methanol vapor.

The electronic absorption spectra of the carbon dot model were simulated using TDDFT and PCM methods. A nitrogen-doped graphene nanoflake with edge functionalization was used as the carbon dot model (Fig.10c and d). The strongest transition of model structure was found at 280 nm, indicating a good match between our model and the true structure of the carbon dots (Fig.10e). The solvation effect led to an increase in the extinction coefficients of carbon dots, compared with the gas phase. These results were consistent with the increase in optical absorbance when the carbon dot film was exposed to solvent vapors in electronic nose. Travlou et al. attributed the sensing mechanism to the chemical reaction between electron-accepting –SO3H groups on carbon dots and electron-donating ammonia [29]. Jiang et al. concluded that the fluorescence quenching of carbon dots arose from the deprotonation of surface carboxyl groups by ammonia [30]. However, the reversibility and fluorescence enhancement of our carbon dot sensor suggested that the sensing was neither due to the ammonia-SO3H interaction nor carboxyl deprotonation, but rather some strong polar-polar interactions. Both DFT and TDDFT/PCM methods thus confirmed that the strong interaction between carbon dots and ammonia led to a change in optical properties, potentially through the hydrophilic surface functional groups and highly polar ammonia molecules.

We have demonstrated a simple, rapid, and real-time detection of ammonia vapor through the use of carbon dot-integrated optical electronic nose. The carbon dots with 2% quantum efficiency were prepared from black sesame seeds using a one-step hydrothermal method. The carbon dots showed spherical shape and nanometer size with excitation-dependence fluorescence emission. The carbon dot film integrated with optical electronic nose demonstrated excellent selectivity and sensitivity towards ammonia vapor. Furthermore, our sensors exhibited relatively high reproducibility as the sensor signal can be returned to its original intensity when the reference gas was switched. Our sensor was able to identify different concentrations of ammonia solutions in water and methanol and differentiate ammonia from ethylenediamine and triethylamine. The ternary solvent systems and an unknown ammonia inhalant were also differentiated using our sensor. The strong interaction between carbon dots and ammonia is responsible for the high sensitivity of our sensor as demonstrated both experimentally and computationally. The paper-based sensor was fabricated to demonstrate the versatility of the carbon dots. Based on this work, we have demonstrated that our carbon dots are multifunctional materials with capabilities of detecting ammonia both in vapor and solution phases, which will benefit a range of industries.

Financial support from the Thailand Research Fund through the Royal Golden Jubilee Ph.D. Program (Grant No. PHD/0130/2559) is provided to P. Supchocksoonthorn and P. Paoprasert. The authors also acknowledge the Central Scientific Instrument Center (CSIC), Thammasat University Center of Scientific Equipment for Advanced Research (TUCSEAR), Department of Chemistry, Department of Biotechnology, Faculty of Science and Technology, and Thammasat University. We acknowledge the partial use of computing resources from the Science Cloud Project at Department of Computer Science, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University and the National e-Science Infrastructure Consortium.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2019.04.087.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
